← the Atlas

hcom vs. kolu — what to adopt

Comparisons·seedling·proposed·JUL 01, 2026

hcom hooks coding agents together through per-tool hooks and a shared SQLite event log — an addressable inbox, event feed, and subscriptions kolu has no equivalent for. But kolu drives agents through the terminal it owns on purpose (agent-agnostic, zero-setup, nothing written to your ~/.claude). The honest borrow is a message channel for the plain text hand-off between turns; it does not remove screen-scraping, because the interactive prompts that matter most — AskUserQuestion, plan approval — are in no log and can't be answered by pasted text.

Contents

  • Two bets on the same problem
  • What a channel buys — and where it stops
  • /debate — the concrete case
  • Where kolu keeps its own bet
  • The smallest first step

A read of hcom — a single Rust binary that “hooks your coding agents together” so they message, watch, and spawn each other across terminals — held against what kolu is. Claims checked against both codebases. hcom and kolu chase the same goal (several coding agents working together across terminals) and bet on opposite substrates, so hcom is a clean reference for the one capability kolu lacks — and a clean foil for the two bets kolu keeps.

Two bets on the same problem

hcom owns no terminal. It rides each tool’s hook system (Claude Code’s PostToolUse/Stop, Gemini, Codex, OpenCode, Cursor, …) and writes everything — messages, status changes, file edits, tool calls — into a local SQLite event log. That one move buys every agent a queryable identity: a name, a status, an inbox, an event feed, subscriptions. Agents @mention each other, tag turns with an intent (request/inform/ack), isolate traffic into threads, and subscribe to events that auto-send a message when they fire. It’s a coordination fabric — agents talk in a channel. The price is stated plainly in hcom’s own README: its hooks “go into config dirs under ~/ on first run,” and every tool needs its own hand-written hook and transcript parser.

kolu made the other bet on purpose, and its README names the two rules that force it:

  • Agent-agnostic — “no adapter to write, no per-agent code… run it once and the next agent that ships works the same way.”
  • Auto-detected, zero setup — “the surface grows with your workflow, not with a preferences pane. If kolu knows something, it’s because the shell already told it.”

So kolu owns the terminal and never touches your ~/.claude. kaval holds every PTY; pulam derives per-terminal awareness — git · PR · agent-state · foreground — as one typed @kolu/surface collection that mirrors over ssh. A superb substrate — with no channel on top. One kolu agent drives another by screen-scraping: the kolu skill types the prompt into a PTY as keystrokes (kaval-tui send), wait --until idle for quiet, then snapshot --viewport to read the reply off the screen. The skill’s own warnings — send is blind, an Enter in the same breath races bracketed-paste and is silently dropped, idle “≠ the reply is correct” — are the tax of coordinating through a screen instead of a channel.

What a channel buys — and where it stops

The borrow is hcom’s shape, not its implementation: no parallel SQLite, no per-tool hook harness. On the surface pulam already serves, add a channel —

  • an inbox Collection + a messages Event, so @name routing is a lookup over keys kolu already broadcasts (mirrored across machines for free by mirrorRemoteSurface);
  • subscriptions over the surface Event primitive — “when @codex finishes, send it the next task”, collision pings, the reactive topologies hcom’s scripts lean on;
  • an intent convention (request/inform/ack) that makes “whose turn” an explicit contract instead of a guess inferred from terminal quiescence;
  • shipped as a skill + workflow scripts — kolu already has the vehicles (the kolu skill, the Workflow tool).

But here is the boundary, and it is the honest heart of this note. A channel cleanly carries the text hand-off between turns — one agent finishes, passes text to the next. It does not abolish screen-scraping, because the state that matters most for coordination is invisible to any log and un-answerable by text:

The interactive-prompt hole — the screen is not optional

When Claude Code blocks on an AskUserQuestion or plan-approval prompt, its Agent SDK buffers that message in memory and flushes it to the JSONL only after you answer — so a log reader sees waiting, never awaiting_user (#905). kolu already had to read the rendered screen to catch it (#1160), matching literal strings (Ready to code?) plus the menu’s shape — a brittle TUI match by nature. And you answer such a menu with arrow keys and Enter aimed at that layout, not pasted text. So a “message as a prompt” can neither see nor answer it. The channel narrows screen-scraping to the interactive prompts; it doesn’t remove it — and hooks don’t rescue this either (below).

One thing kolu does not lose by refusing hcom’s hooks: mid-turn delivery. hcom can drop a message between an agent’s tool calls; kolu, delivering into a PTY at its prompt, is between-turns only. But turn-based coordination — debate, worker-reviewer, pipeline — wants each agent to finish before the next message lands. Between-turns is the correct semantics, not a limitation.

/debate — the concrete case

/debate already solved the “don’t read big content off a screen” problem — with the filesystem. Agents exchange arguments as numbered turn-files in the shared repo directory: each reads the others’ files and writes its own by path. The skill is explicit — “pass file paths, not pasted argument text — the agents read and write the shared directory directly.” So the arguments never touch the screen. kaval-tui send only pings the next agent (“your turn — read <k−1>.*.md, write <k>.<id>.md”), and the screen is read only to confirm a turn finished and its file landed.

That means a channel has exactly two things to tighten here — both coordination, both modest, because the content is already handled by files:

  • The ping lands reliably. Today it’s a blind kaval-tui send plus a separate --key Enter that can race bracketed-paste and silently drop. An addressed request message just arrives — “whose turn” as an explicit hand-off, not a keystroke that might not submit.
  • A clean done-signal. Today the orchestrator waits for the screen to settle, snapshots, and verifies the file exists. The agent messaging “done — wrote 03.foo.md” is a push instead of that infer-from-the-screen-then-check-disk dance, so an orchestrated round advances the moment a turn lands.

That’s the whole benefit for /debate: content already flows through files, so the channel only cleans up the ping and the done-signal — it doesn’t change how arguments move, and it does nothing for a debater that stops mid-turn on an AskUserQuestion (still screen-bound, to both see and answer). The borrow is also scoped to terminal debates — /lens-debate (lowy⇄hickey) and /codex-debate run as in-process Agent/Workflow subagents, never touch a PTY, and have nothing to screen-scrape.

Where kolu keeps its own bet

Two places the comparison says don’t copy hcom.

  • Own the terminal; don’t touch the config. keep hcom’s channel is bought by hooks in your ~/.claude and bespoke code per tool — exactly what kolu’s two rules forbid. #905 is that philosophy in one frame: kolu had the option to detect AskUserQuestion via a PreToolUse hook and deliberately chose to read the rendered screen instead — “detect by scraping the rendered screen — zero-config” — accepting the brittleness as the price of never reconfiguring your agent. That’s not kolu lagging hcom; it’s kolu refusing hcom’s setup tax. (And it’s why a hook wouldn’t rescue the hole above: it would spot the awaiting call earlier than the log, but still can’t answer the menu with a message — so it costs the config-touch and buys only half.)

  • Typed surface-over-ssh, not a shared-PSK MQTT relay. keep hcom’s cross-device story is a shared-secret MQTT relay on public brokers, and its README is honest about the cost: “enrollment is total trust… treat that as shell access on every enrolled device,” with no forward secrecy and no revocation. kolu’s path is @kolu/surface-nix-host over ssh: per-connection OS auth, no third-party broker, no single secret whose leak is game-over. Adopt the channel; keep the transport — the mailbox rides mirrorRemoteSurface over that same ssh link, and the relay never gets built.

  • First-party PTYs and the fold discipline stay. The herdr bet (kolu owns the terminal, so live screen, scrollback, resize, remote survival are guarantees) and kolu’s fold model (a memoryless producer whose events one owner folds into stored state) are load-bearing. A message surface honors the fold: messages are occurrences on an Event, folded into an inbox by the receiver — not a shared table many writers mutate.

The smallest first step

One PR-sized proof, scoped to the path that doesn’t exist yet — an agent addressing another by name over the surface, not by typing into its screen. Add a messages Event and an inbox Collection to the pulam surface; a kolu-tui msg @name -- <text> verb and a kolu-tui inbox [--wait]; deliver into a terminal that’s at its prompt. The done-criterion that can only be met by the real path: two agents on two hosts exchange a request/ack through the surface — mirrored over ssh by mirrorRemoteSurface, no keystroke injected and no screen scraped for that hand-off. State the boundary in the same breath: interactive prompts (AskUserQuestion, plan approval, permission dialogs) stay screen-read — a separate, unsolved problem this does not close. Everything above the channel — subscriptions, collision detection, ensemble scripts — is additive once it exists.
